questions.specification:
A manufacturer of high-end induction cooktops for professional kitchens has developed a new prototype featuring a haptic-feedback control system. Before the final design is approved for production, the company invites a group of senior executive chefs and industrial safety consultants to evaluate the prototype. The table below displays the mean scores collected during these evaluation sessions.

| Design Criterion | Mean Rating: Senior Chefs ($$0-10$$) | Mean Rating: Safety Consultants ($$0-10$$) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Heat Regulation Accuracy | $$9.6$$ | $$8.4$$ |
| Surface Material Durability | $$8.1$$ | $$9.2$$ |
| Emergency Shutdown Speed | $$7.5$$ | $$9.8$$ |
| Interface Accessibility | $$8.9$$ | $$7.2$$ |

Which market research method is primarily being used by the manufacturer to refine this product design?

options.markscheme: Incorrect. A user trial typically involves testing by members of the intended target market (the general public or non-expert consumers) in a real-world setting, rather than a formal evaluation by specialists.

Correct. Expert appraisal involves specialists in a particular field (in this case, senior chefs and safety consultants) providing an informed evaluation of a product based on their professional knowledge and experience.
Incorrect. Trend analysis involves examining historical market data or consumer behavior over time to predict future developments, which is not what is occurring in this prototype evaluation.
Incorrect. A literature search is a form of secondary research involving the collection of existing data from publications, journals, or reports, rather than primary testing of a prototype.

questions.specification:
A start-up, **PulsePod**, is developing a refillable insulated bottle that tracks hydration and sends reminders through an app. The team is unsure which customer segment to prioritise and which features justify a higher price.

Before committing to tooling, PulsePod ran a **two‑stage market research plan**:

- **Stage 1 (exploratory):** 12 online interviews with runners, office workers and students.
- **Stage 2 (quantitative):** a survey of **240** potential customers recruited through social media ads.

The survey results are summarised below.

| Finding | Result |
|---|---:|
| Would consider buying a bottle in the next 3 months | 58% |
| Most important purchase driver | Leak-proof lid (34%), keeps drinks cold (29%), price (21%), app reminders (16%) |
| Most important purchase driver by segment | Runners: keeps drinks cold (38%), leak-proof lid (33%), price (17%), app reminders (12%); Office workers: leak-proof lid (39%), price (24%), keeps drinks cold (22%), app reminders (15%); Students: price (36%), leak-proof lid (28%), app reminders (20%), keeps drinks cold (16%) |
| Preferred buying channel | Online marketplace (44%), brand website (31%), sports store (25%) |
| Acceptable price range (most common) | $25$–$35$ |

PulsePod’s draft concept sheet (used in interviews and the survey) included:
- three colour options (black, mint, sand)
- two lid types (flip-top, straw)
- optional app subscription ($1.99/month$) for reminders and weekly reports

PulsePod’s investor asks for evidence that the product will meet real customer needs and that the marketing plan is based on reliable research.

![concept sheet showing a smart insulated bottle with two lid options, three colours, and an app subscription note](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/ef08de98-496e-45c0-9bb6-d5412b72b474.png)

Figure 1: PulsePod draft concept sheet

![survey dashboard with bar charts for drivers, buying channel, and a price range band highlighted $25$–$35$](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/1cf8a34f-2118-4852-8ac5-7c1f2055a1da.png)

Figure 2: Summary of PulsePod survey results

parts.content: State two purposes of market research for a company at PulsePod’s stage of product development.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies/validates customer needs and preferences to reduce risk of developing the wrong product <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Estimates demand/market size or likelihood of purchase to support viability/forecasting <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Informs pricing decisions (acceptable price range/willingness to pay) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Informs target market selection/segmentation and positioning <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Informs route-to-market/marketing mix (channels, promotion messages) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how PulsePod could use the Stage 2 survey findings to guide two design decisions for the product or service.
parts.markscheme:
- Prioritise design features aligned to key drivers (e.g., improve leak-proofing/lid sealing; enhance insulation performance) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Select lid type(s) or offer variants informed by stated preferences/importance of leak-proofing (e.g., refine flip-top or straw for leak resistance) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Adjust product/app offer because app reminders are lowest driver (e.g., make app optional, simplify, or bundle rather than lead with it) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Align specification/finishes to the price band $25$–$35$ by reducing/adding features to hit target cost-value balance <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline two pieces of design guidance (requirements) PulsePod should apply when creating the questionnaire for Stage 2 to improve the quality of the data.
parts.markscheme:
- Use clear, unambiguous wording and define terms (e.g., what “consider buying” means; timeframe) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Avoid leading/loaded questions; keep questions neutral <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Use appropriate question types and response scales (e.g., consistent Likert scales; mutually exclusive options) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pilot the questionnaire to identify misunderstandings/biased wording and improve reliability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensure logical order/flow and limit length to reduce respondent fatigue <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Include demographic/segment questions needed for analysis (e.g., age, activity type) while complying with ethics/privacy <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how PulsePod could apply two different market research strategies to reduce uncertainty before investing in tooling and launch.
parts.markscheme:
- Use focus groups or additional interviews with defined segments to explore motivations, barriers, and language for positioning (qualitative insight to refine concept) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use a concept test/A-B test of feature bundles (e.g., with/without subscription; lid types) to quantify preference trade-offs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use prototype/user testing (observational research) to verify usability and validate leak-proof/insulation claims in real contexts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use competitor/market desk research to benchmark price points, claims, and differentiation and identify gaps <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use a small-scale pilot launch or crowdfunding pre-orders to validate willingness to pay and demand with real purchase behaviour <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 5 marks</note>

parts.content: Discuss how PulsePod should interpret and use its market research to decide (i) a primary target segment and (ii) an initial marketing mix (product, price, place, promotion).
parts.markscheme:
- Segments the data (e.g., runners vs office workers vs students) and selects a primary segment based on strongest need-fit and purchase intent <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Justifies segment choice using evidence (e.g., segment prioritises leak-proof/insulation; aligns with app value; higher willingness to pay) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Product: prioritises features supported by findings (leak-proofing, cold retention) and positions app as supporting feature or optional add-on <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Price: sets initial price within/around $25$–$35$ and explains how this supports adoption and margin; considers tiering/bundles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Place: focuses on online marketplace and brand website as primary channels, reflecting stated preferences; considers sports retail selectively <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Promotion: develops messages around top drivers (leak-proof, cold) and uses language learned from qualitative research; selects channels aligned to segment (e.g., social ads/influencers if runners) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evaluates limitations of the research (sampling via social ads, self-report bias, sample size, representativeness) and states how to mitigate (triangulation, further research) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how insights will be fed back into iterative design decisions and success metrics (e.g., conversion rate, return rate, subscription uptake) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Makes a balanced judgement, referencing evidence and acknowledging uncertainty <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 9 marks</note>

questions.specification: A toy manufacturer employs a certified safety consultant to evaluate the structural integrity and material toxicity of a new prototype. Which research method is being used?
Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of a well-defined marketing specification?

A toy manufacturer evaluates the growth of online gaming versus traditional board games over the last decade to decide where to invest in new product lines. Which research technique is being used to identify these long-term shifts in consumer behavior?

**RefillBuddy** plans to launch a reusable household-cleaning spray bottle that customers can refill using concentrated tablets dissolved in water. The founders want to reduce plastic waste and are unsure which customer group to target first and how to position the product.

They have a limited budget for research and need evidence to decide:
- whether there is sufficient demand
- what features matter most (e.g., durability, aesthetics, ease of refilling, child safety lock)
- which messages and channels will be most effective

A concept render is shown in Figure 1.

![A simple product render of a reusable spray bottle with a screw-off base, a measurement window on the side, and a small tablet container clipped to the neck.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/e8d7a1f1-f55c-438f-82c4-cc0babd421b2.png)

Figure 1: RefillBuddy concept

parts.content: State two reasons why RefillBuddy should carry out market research before committing to large-scale production and launch.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduce risk by checking if enough customers would buy / size of demand <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify customer needs/wants to inform product specifications (e.g., safety, ease of refill) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Test or refine positioning/USP and marketing messages before launch <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Support decision-making for target market selection/segmentation and resource allocation <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline how RefillBuddy could decide which customer segment to target first for the product launch.
parts.markscheme:
- Use **secondary research** (e.g., industry reports, competitor reviews, retail data) to identify likely segments and estimate market size <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use **primary research** (e.g., survey/interviews) to compare segments on purchase intent, price sensitivity, and key purchase drivers, then select the strongest segment <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
- Run a small **test market/pilot** (e.g., one city or one online audience) and compare conversion/retention between segments to choose a target <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline two questionnaire design guidelines RefillBuddy should follow to test customer interest and feature preferences.
parts.markscheme:
- Use clear, neutral wording (avoid leading/loaded questions) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Keep questions specific and unambiguous; avoid double-barrelled questions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provide appropriate response formats (e.g., Likert scales) with balanced options and a ‘not applicable/other’ where needed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Order questions logically; start broad then narrow; include screening questions for relevant respondents <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how RefillBuddy could reduce uncertainty about demand and refine the product and marketing approach before launch.
parts.markscheme:
- Use a **mixed-method plan** (secondary + primary) to triangulate findings and reduce uncertainty <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Collect **secondary data** (e.g., trends in refill/zero-waste, competitor pricing/reviews, retailer category growth) to estimate demand and identify gaps/opportunities <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Run **primary research** (e.g., concept tests, focus groups, prototype trials) to discover which features/messages drive purchase and what barriers exist (e.g., convenience concerns) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use results to make evidence-based decisions: adjust features/packaging/claims, choose target segment and channels, and validate willingness-to-pay before scaling production <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Who is the main stakeholder accountable for championing a new product and ensuring it aligns with market needs and preferences throughout its development phase?

**AquaPure**

A new company, AquaPure, aims to introduce a line of advanced water filtration systems for home use. These systems are designed to be eco-friendly, easy to install, and cost-effective. AquaPure wants to ensure that their product meets consumer needs and stands out in a competitive market. 

They are focusing on sustainable materials, innovative filtration technology, and user-friendly design. AquaPure needs to conduct thorough market research to ensure the product meets consumer needs and preferences.

parts.content: Identify **two** market research strategies that AquaPure could use to gather information about consumer preferences for water filtration systems.
parts.markscheme:
- Use surveys/questionnaires to collect data on desired features, material preferences and acceptable price range <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use focus groups to gather qualitative feedback through discussion of preferences and opinions about water filtration systems <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how AquaPure could use perceptual mapping to position their water filtration system in the market.
parts.markscheme:
- Define perceptual mapping as a visual representation of consumer perceptions of products/brands in a market <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that AquaPure plots competitor products against key attributes (e.g., price, sustainability, filtration performance) to see relative positioning and gaps <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Link the identified gap/desired position to how AquaPure can emphasise differentiating features in its marketing/design to appeal to the target market <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe **two** advantages of conducting a competitor analysis for AquaPure before launching their water filtration system.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify market opportunities by comparing competitors’ strengths/weaknesses to find ways to differentiate (features, quality, sustainability, price) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduce risk/improve decision-making by informing pricing, marketing and product features based on competitor strategies and positioning <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: AquaPure decides to conduct a usability testing session for their new water filtration system design. Outline the key steps they should follow in this process.
parts.markscheme:
- Define the objectives/criteria for the test (e.g., ease of installation, ease of use, satisfaction) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recruit suitable participants from the target market and prepare realistic tasks/scenarios <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Run the session: brief participants, observe them completing tasks, and record issues/feedback <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Analyse findings, prioritise problems, and implement improvements (iterate if needed) <marks>1 mark</marks>
